title: Tetrate AI Gateway for Apigee & Java Developers
subtitle: Govern LLMs and AI agents at the edge — every concept anchored to Apigee and Spring Boot
author: Mayari edition

parts.title: Part 1 — Why an AI gateway exists
parts.theme: What an AI gateway is, why direct LLM calls stop scaling, and Tetrate's lay of the land.

parts.title: Part 2 — The unified multi-provider gateway
parts.theme: One API in front of every model: backends, credentials, routing, failover.

parts.title: Part 3 — Governance: tokens, cost & quotas
parts.theme: Count tokens not requests; budgets, cost attribution, sanctioned models, caching.

parts.title: Part 4 — Security & guardrails
parts.theme: Authenticate callers, defend prompts, redact PII, moderate output, secure the upstream.

parts.title: Part 5 — The agentic turn: MCP & tools
parts.theme: From chat to agents: MCP gateways, tool routing, securing autonomous workflows.

parts.title: Part 6 — Observability & operations
parts.theme: See, debug, deploy, and operate AI traffic in production.

parts.title: Part 7 — Capstone
parts.theme: Assemble a governed, observable, multi-provider agent platform on Tetrate.

1.title: What an AI gateway is — and why 'just call OpenAI' stops working
1.objective: Explain what an AI gateway adds in front of LLM/agent traffic, and why calling provider SDKs directly from each service stops scaling the moment you have many apps, models, and a finance team.
1.apigee_bridge: An AI gateway is an API-management product for LLM traffic — Apigee for tokens, prompts, and tools instead of REST resources.
1.java_bridge: It's the edge that absorbs the cross-cutting concerns you'd otherwise re-code around every `OpenAiChatClient` / RestClient call.
1.stretch: Inventory every place your services call an LLM today and list which concerns (keys, retries, cost, logging) each re-implements.

2.title: The AI gateway vs the API gateway you know
2.objective: Map the AI gateway mental model onto the Apigee one you already have — and pin down the handful of things that are genuinely new because the payload is tokens, prompts, and tool calls.
2.apigee_bridge: Proxy→AIGatewayRoute, TargetServer→AIServiceBackend, Quota→token rate limit, API Product→model access — most of Apigee transfers, with new units.
2.java_bridge: Same instinct as moving cross-cutting logic out of a Spring service into a gateway — but the 'request' is now a chat completion.
2.stretch: Draw a two-column table mapping five Apigee objects to their AI-gateway equivalents, and mark which has no clean analogue.

3.title: Tetrate's AI landscape: TARS, Agent Router Enterprise & Envoy AI Gateway
3.objective: Place Tetrate's AI products on a map — the managed Agent Router Service (TARS), Agent Router Enterprise, Agent Operations Director, and the open-source Envoy AI Gateway they all build on — and pick your on-ramp.
3.apigee_bridge: Like choosing between Apigee SaaS, hybrid, and the underlying runtime — managed service vs self-hosted control of the same engine.
3.java_bridge: TARS is the 'Spring Boot starter' on-ramp: a hosted endpoint you point at; self-hosting is owning the deployment yourself.
3.stretch: Decide which Tetrate tier fits your org today and write the one constraint that would push you to the next tier.

4.title: Hello, gateway: TARS as a drop-in OpenAI endpoint
4.objective: Make your first governed LLM call by swapping the OpenAI base URL for the Tetrate Agent Router Service endpoint — no provider keys in your app, one line changed.
4.apigee_bridge: Your first passthrough — point a client at the gateway host instead of the backend, exactly like calling an Apigee proxy basepath.
4.java_bridge: Change `spring.ai.openai.base-url` (or the OpenAI Java SDK base URL) to the router; your code is otherwise untouched.
4.stretch: Point a small Spring AI app at TARS and confirm the same code now runs against two different model providers by name.

5.title: Self-host it: Envoy AI Gateway's three core resources
5.objective: Stand up a self-hosted AI gateway on Kubernetes and read the three resources that define it: AIServiceBackend (the provider), BackendSecurityPolicy (the credentials), and AIGatewayRoute (the route).
5.apigee_bridge: The bundle anatomy of the AI world: AIGatewayRoute is the ProxyEndpoint, AIServiceBackend is the TargetServer, BackendSecurityPolicy is the credential.
5.java_bridge: Declarative Kubernetes CRDs instead of `@Bean` wiring — the gateway is configured, not coded, like Spring config moved to YAML.
5.stretch: Apply the three CRDs against a local kind cluster with the aigw CLI and route a hello-world completion through them.

6.title: One API, many providers: the OpenAI-compatible schema
6.objective: Use a single OpenAI-compatible API to reach many providers, and define each provider with an AIServiceBackend and its API schema.
6.apigee_bridge: AIServiceBackend is a TargetServer plus a schema adapter — it normalises each provider's API to one client-facing contract.
6.java_bridge: One SDK, many backends: like a single `ChatClient` interface with swappable implementations, but resolved at the edge.
6.stretch: Add a second provider backend (e.g. Anthropic or Bedrock) behind the same OpenAI-compatible route and call both.

7.title: Provider credentials & secrets: BackendSecurityPolicy
7.objective: Keep provider API keys out of application code by configuring upstream credentials with BackendSecurityPolicy, and understand how the gateway injects them.
7.apigee_bridge: This is the gateway holding the backend credential — like Apigee target auth / a KVM secret the proxy injects, not the client.
7.java_bridge: Delete the `OPENAI_API_KEY` from your app config; the gateway owns it now, the way a sidecar/secret manager would.
7.stretch: Move a hard-coded provider key out of a Spring app into a BackendSecurityPolicy and confirm the app no longer needs it.

8.title: Model routing & the approved model catalog
8.objective: Route requests to the right model by name and expose a curated catalog of approved models through AIGatewayRoute rules.
8.apigee_bridge: Model-name routing is RouteRules + conditions keyed on the request — and the approved catalog is an API Product for models.
8.java_bridge: Stop hard-coding model strings across services; the catalog is the single place 'which models exist' lives, like a config server.
8.stretch: Define a virtual model name that maps to a concrete provider model, and switch the backing model with no client change.

9.title: Fallback, retries & failover across providers
9.objective: Keep AI traffic flowing when a provider degrades by configuring retries, timeouts, and cross-provider fallback at the gateway.
9.apigee_bridge: Gateway-level resilience like Apigee TargetServers with a LoadBalancer and health — but failing over to a different model vendor.
9.java_bridge: The Resilience4j retry/circuit-breaker you'd wrap around an LLM call, lifted out of every service into one policy.
9.stretch: Force a primary provider to fail and prove the gateway transparently serves the request from a fallback model.

10.title: Beyond chat: embeddings, multimodal & other endpoints
10.objective: Route non-chat AI traffic — embeddings, multimodal, and other endpoints — through the same governed gateway.
10.apigee_bridge: Different operations on the same product: embeddings vs chat are distinct paths/methods scoped by the route, like proxy resources.
10.java_bridge: Your `EmbeddingClient` and `ChatClient` both point at the gateway; vector pipelines get the same governance for free.
10.stretch: Send an embeddings request and a chat request through the gateway and confirm both are metered and governed.

11.title: Token-based rate limiting: count tokens, not requests
11.objective: Protect cost and capacity by rate-limiting on tokens — input, output, and total — per model and per user, using BackendTrafficPolicy and the gateway's token metering.
11.apigee_bridge: Apigee Quota, but the unit is tokens not calls: the gateway reads usage from the LLM response and counts that, keyed by model+user.
11.java_bridge: The per-pod Bucket4j/Resilience4j limiter you can't make accurate for variable-cost calls — done right, distributed, on real token usage.
11.stretch: Set a low per-user token budget and watch a long completion get throttled while short ones pass.

12.title: Budgets & cost attribution per user, team & app
12.objective: Attribute spend to the right user, team, and application and enforce budgets — the core of Tetrate Agent Operations Director.
12.apigee_bridge: Cost attribution is Apigee Analytics + monetization for tokens: spend per developer/app/product, with budgets as enforced quotas.
12.java_bridge: The chargeback/showback you'd hand-build from Micrometer counters — turned into first-class per-identity cost governance.
12.stretch: Tag requests with a team identity and produce a per-team token-spend breakdown from the gateway.

13.title: Sanctioned-model governance & access tiers
13.objective: Control which callers may use which models — sanctioned-model enforcement, tiers, and deny-by-default access.
13.apigee_bridge: An API Product for models: a tier bundles the models, limits, and scopes a given app is entitled to call.
13.java_bridge: Centralises the 'is this team allowed the expensive model?' check you'd otherwise scatter through service code and config.
13.stretch: Create two tiers (cheap-only vs full catalog) and prove a cheap-tier key is refused the premium model.

14.title: Caching to cut cost & latency
14.objective: Reduce spend and latency with response, prompt, and semantic caching at the gateway, and know when each is safe.
14.apigee_bridge: ResponseCache for AI: cache completions/embeddings — but the cache key and 'is this safe to cache' question are new for prompts.
14.java_bridge: The Spring `@Cacheable` you'd put around an embedding call, moved to the edge and shared across every service.
14.stretch: Cache an embeddings call and measure the latency and cost difference on a repeated identical request.

15.title: Authenticating callers at the AI edge
15.objective: Establish who is calling the gateway — API keys, JWT, and OAuth for client applications — before any model runs.
15.apigee_bridge: VerifyAPIKey / OAuthV2 / VerifyJWT, unchanged in spirit — caller identity is the gate before token limits and budgets apply.
15.java_bridge: The Spring Security filter chain in front of your controllers, now in front of every LLM and tool call instead.
15.stretch: Require a JWT with a specific scope to reach the gateway and reject an unauthenticated call.

16.title: AI guardrails: prompt-injection & jailbreak defense
16.objective: Inspect and filter prompts to defend against prompt injection and jailbreak attempts at the gateway, before they reach a model.
16.apigee_bridge: Threat protection for prompts: the JSON/regex/threat policies you know, but inspecting natural-language attack patterns.
16.java_bridge: Input validation you can't fully express with `@Valid` — a guardrail layer for free-text the model would otherwise trust.
16.stretch: Craft an injection attempt and confirm the guardrail blocks it while a benign prompt passes.

17.title: PII redaction & data-loss prevention
17.objective: Detect and redact sensitive data in prompts and responses so PII never reaches a provider or a log.
17.apigee_bridge: Data masking, raised a level: not just hiding values in traces but redacting PII from the payload sent upstream.
17.java_bridge: The log-scrubbing and DLP you'd bolt onto a service, applied to every prompt and completion at one chokepoint.
17.stretch: Send a prompt containing a fake card number and confirm it is redacted before egress and in logs.

18.title: Content safety & output moderation
18.objective: Moderate model output — content safety categories, blocking, and safe-completion policies — on the response path.
18.apigee_bridge: A response-flow policy that shapes/blocks output, like a PostFlow content check — but judging generated text, not a schema.
18.java_bridge: The `ResponseBodyAdvice` you'd write to vet output, replaced by a moderation guardrail you don't maintain per service.
18.stretch: Configure an output category to block and confirm an unsafe completion is withheld from the caller.

19.title: Securing the upstream: provider auth, mTLS & egress
19.objective: Lock down the gateway-to-provider leg: upstream authentication, mTLS, and controlled egress to approved providers only.
19.apigee_bridge: South-bound TLS/mTLS and TargetServers — the same backend-trust story, now for LLM providers and tool servers.
19.java_bridge: The `RestClient` truststore and egress allow-list you'd configure per service, centralised at the gateway.
19.stretch: Restrict egress so the gateway can reach only your approved provider hosts and prove a rogue host is blocked.

20.title: From chat to agents: what changes for the gateway
20.objective: Understand how agentic traffic — tool calling, multi-step loops, and long-running sessions — differs from a single chat completion, and what the gateway must now govern.
20.apigee_bridge: An agent call is a conversation of requests, not one — closer to orchestrating several proxy calls than serving a single one.
20.java_bridge: Like the difference between one `@GetMapping` and a saga of service calls — the gateway now governs the whole loop.
20.stretch: Trace a tool-calling agent run and count how many gateway round-trips one user prompt actually produces.

21.title: MCP gateways: the MCPRoute resource
21.objective: Put a gateway in front of Model Context Protocol tool servers using MCPRoute — routing, security, and observability for tool calls.
21.apigee_bridge: MCPRoute is an AIGatewayRoute for tools: parentRefs, path, backendRefs, securityPolicy — the same shape, a new backend kind.
21.java_bridge: Your agent's tools become governed backends instead of raw HTTP clients scattered through the app.
21.stretch: Front a sample MCP server with an MCPRoute and call one of its tools through the gateway.

22.title: Tool routing, aggregation & multiplexing
22.objective: Expose many MCP servers as one surface — server multiplexing, tool-name prefixing, and selective tool exposure with toolSelector.
22.apigee_bridge: Aggregating backends behind one route, like an Apigee facade proxy — the gateway prefixes tools (github__, context7__) to disambiguate.
22.java_bridge: One tool registry for the agent instead of N injected clients; the gateway is the service-discovery layer.
22.stretch: Aggregate two MCP servers and use toolSelector to expose only a safe subset of their tools.

23.title: Securing tools: OAuth & fine-grained authorization
23.objective: Protect tool invocations with OAuth per the MCP authorization spec and fine-grained rules over which identities may call which tools.
23.apigee_bridge: securityPolicy.oauth + authorization.rules is OAuthV2 + operation scoping for tools — JWT scopes and CEL deciding tool access.
23.java_bridge: Method-level security (`@PreAuthorize`) for tools the agent can invoke, enforced at the edge rather than in each tool.
23.stretch: Require a scope to call a write tool and confirm a read-only token is refused it via a CEL rule.

24.title: Agent identity & guardrails for autonomous workflows
24.objective: Give agents an identity, propagate it across tool calls, and apply guardrails and budgets to autonomous, multi-step workflows.
24.apigee_bridge: An agent is a first-class consumer like a developer app — identity, entitlement, quota, and audit follow it through the loop.
24.java_bridge: Propagating a security/trace context across a call chain — but the chain is an autonomous agent the gateway must keep on a leash.
24.stretch: Attach a per-agent token budget and prove a runaway agent loop is cut off when it exhausts the budget.

25.title: Observability for AI traffic: tokens, latency & tracing
25.objective: See what your AI traffic is doing — token usage, latency, cost, and OpenInference traces of LLM and agent calls.
25.apigee_bridge: Apigee Analytics for AI: the built-in dashboards and traces, but the dimensions are model, tokens, and tool calls.
25.java_bridge: Micrometer/Actuator metrics and distributed traces you'd wire per service, captured at the edge with OpenInference semantics.
25.stretch: Open a trace of an agent run and read the per-step token and latency breakdown.

26.title: Debugging AI requests at the gateway
26.objective: Diagnose a failing or misbehaving AI request — inspect the request as transformed, the provider response, and where a guardrail or limit fired.
26.apigee_bridge: The Apigee Trace/Debug tool for AI: step the request through routing, guardrails, and the provider call to find what fired.
26.java_bridge: Your debugger and request logs, but for a call that left your process — the gateway is where you set the breakpoint now.
26.stretch: Reproduce a blocked request and use the gateway's debug view to identify exactly which policy rejected it.

27.title: Deploying & operating: Kubernetes, Gateway API & the aigw CLI
27.objective: Operate the gateway as config-as-code — Gateway API + the AI CRDs, the aigw CLI for local dev, and promotion across clusters.
27.apigee_bridge: Config-as-code and environment promotion, Apigee-style, but the artifacts are Kubernetes CRDs travelling a GitOps pipeline.
27.java_bridge: Your app's CI/CD pipeline and profiles, applied to gateway config; the aigw CLI is your local run before you ship.
27.stretch: Run the gateway locally with the aigw CLI, then promote the same manifests to a cluster unchanged.

28.title: Production readiness: SLOs, rollout & cost dashboards
28.objective: Run a go-live review for an AI gateway — SLOs for latency and availability, safe rollout of model/policy changes, cost dashboards, and on-call.
28.apigee_bridge: The production-readiness checklist from operating Apigee, extended with AI-specific items: token budgets, guardrails, model rollout.
28.java_bridge: The pre-launch readiness review you'd run before flipping a feature flag on a payments service — for the AI edge.
28.stretch: Write SLOs for your gateway and a safe-rollout plan for swapping a model version behind a virtual model name.

29.title: Capstone: a governed multi-provider agent platform on Tetrate
29.objective: Assemble everything — multi-provider routing, token budgets, guardrails, MCP tools, and observability — into one governed agent platform, and run a readiness review.
29.apigee_bridge: The whole platform: every Apigee-anchored concept composed into one operated AI edge serving real agents.
29.java_bridge: Every Spring-bridge from the course in one deployment — the AI platform your services and agents all call through.
29.stretch: Stand up the full platform and run an end-to-end agent workflow that exercises routing, budgets, guardrails, and a tool call.
